repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem:
- Builtin "Vimscript" functions (f_xx) are mostly implemented in C. Partly that's because there is some boilerplate required to call out to Lua.
- Calls to `vim.fn.foo()` always marshall over the Lua <=> Vimscript ("typval") bridge, even if `fn.foo()` is implemented entirely in Lua: 
  ```
  Lua => typval => Object => Lua => Object => typval => Lua.
  ```

## Solution:

 Functions declared with `func_lua` in `eval.lua` are implemented in entirely in Lua (`_core/vimfn.lua`), not C.

- `gen_eval.lua` wires `func_lua` entries to `lua_wrapper`, which handles the typval conversion for Vimscript callers (slow path).
- `nlua_call()` detects `func_lua` functions and calls the Lua implementation directly. This eliminates all conversion overhead for Lua callers (fast path).
- Validate at build-time that `func`, `func_float`, and `func_lua` are mutually exclusive.
- Migrate `hostname()` as a toy example, to show the idea.

## Note

- I'm not sure how useful this will be, but I want us to try it out. It's a minor change with negligible downside and maybe-significant upside.
- I will followup with a similar change for excmds, which is more obviously useful for things like `:log`.

cc @bfredl @lewis6991

comment_regular_data.body:
`vim.fn.*` mainly has the problems:
- functions that return boolean return `0|1` which is a huge footgun, e.g. when doing `if vim.fn.has(...) then`
- Issues with lua-string -> vim-string|vim-blob conversion (as lua strings are not nul terminated).
- Issues with table -> vim-dict|vim-array conversion
- And perf which you've mentioned

Functions that mostly deal with integer/numbers don't really have any issues.

> I'm not sure how useful this will be, but I want us to try it out. It's a minor change with negligible downside and maybe-significant upside.

Functions like `hostname` also don't really have any issues afaik, but are the easiest to implement with this scheme, so I'm not sure it's really worth it. I think we'd need to implement a function that we know may be used in a hot path and would materially benefit from a lua implementation.

This is worth it because there's almost no cost, plus the cleanup in `src/nvim/eval.lua` improves readability. It wasn't clear before that `float_func`, `api_wrapper`, and `func`, were all closely related or mutually exclusive.

We can always drop `func_lua` later if needed. But this reduces excuses in the future, and lays groundwork for other improvements that I am planning.

> This is worth it because there's almost no cost

How do you determine that? Surely the cost is proportional to the size of each lua implementation. For anything that's worth it, this could be non-trivial. Otherwise for functions like hostname, it's almost no cost, for almost no gain, so a wash basically.

For now I'm just guessing, and will find out later. Because there is no cost to trying this.

If the Lua impls are somehow more costly than the C impls, that usually indicates that Lua is missing things that it needs, and then we might want to implement those things. That is the purose of the API, after all. Not to mention, Lua reduces the chance of segfaults.

https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38116 is an example of the same kind of idea.

The way I look at it, you are going down a route that minimizes surface area, but at the cost of having two implementations work functionally identically in lock step. To me that seems quite high cost.

> If the Lua impls are somehow more costly than the C impls, that usually indicates that Lua is missing things that it needs

It's not about the Lua impls being more costly than the C impls. Most of the C impls are just typval building with a normal C function sitting in the middle. The Lua impls would probably use that same internal function but have lots of lua specific builder logic around it (using the Lua-C API).

### Proposal 

What I think is more worthwhile would be to **REPLACE** these C/typval impls with the Lua version. The vim functions are already super slow, and no one uses vimscript anymore, so doesn't matter if they become slower. And is more in the spirit of #38116.

> **REPLACE** these C/typval impls with the Lua version.

That is what this does... there's no duplication. The C impl of `f_hostname` is removed.

The initial goal here is to (1) avoid future C implementations where possible, and (2) see how close we can get to replacing some existing vim.fn functions with Lua.

Ohh. Sorry. I misunderstood the PR description and missed the removal of `f_hostname` in the diff (since it's so small). It looked like you added another implementation which is more efficient for `vim.fn.*`, whilst keeping the old one.

I'm all for this 👍

> I think we'd need to implement a function that we know may be used in a hot path and would materially benefit from a lua implementation.

Maybe interesting:

```lua
local api = vim.api
local uv = vim.uv

---@param pos string
---@param win? integer
---@return integer
local function lua_fn_line(pos, win)
    win = win or 0
    local buf = win == 0 and 0 or api.nvim_win_get_buf(win)

    local b1 = string.byte(pos, 1)
    if b1 == 46 then -- '.'
        return api.nvim_win_get_cursor(win)[1]
    elseif b1 == 36 then -- '$'
        return api.nvim_buf_line_count(buf)
    elseif b1 == 39 then -- '
        return api.nvim_buf_get_mark(buf, string.sub(pos, 2))[1]
    elseif b1 == 34 then -- "
        return api.nvim_buf_get_mark(buf, '"')[1]
    end

    error("Unsupported position for lua_fn_line_byte: " .. vim.inspect(pos))
end

-- mark 'a

local function iter_lua_fn()
    local positions = { ".", "$", "'a", ".", "$", "'b", ".", "$", '"' }
    local iterations = 2000000
    local total_calls = iterations * #positions

    local total_vim = 0
    local total_lua = 0

    for _ = 1, iterations do
        for _, pos in ipairs(positions) do
            local start = uv.hrtime()
            local _ = vim.call("line", pos)
            local stop = uv.hrtime()
            total_vim = total_vim + (stop - start)
        end
    end

    for _ = 1, iterations do
        for _, pos in ipairs(positions) do
            local start = uv.hrtime()
            local _ = lua_fn_line(pos)
            local stop = uv.hrtime()
            total_lua = total_lua + (stop - start)
        end
    end

    local vim_ns = total_vim / total_calls
    local byte_ns = total_lua / total_calls

    -- mark 'b

    print("\n=== line() Performance Comparison ===")
    print(string.format("Positions     : %s", table.concat(positions, ", ")))
    print(string.format("Total calls   : %d", total_calls))
    print(
        "────────────────────────────────────"
    )
    print(
        string.format("vim.call      : %7.2f µs total  (%6.0f ns/call)", total_vim / 1000, vim_ns)
    )
    print(
        string.format("lua_fn      : %7.2f µs total  (%6.0f ns/call)", total_lua / 1000, byte_ns)
    )
    print(
        "────────────────────────────────────"
    )
    print(string.format("lua_fn vs vim : %.2fx faster", vim_ns / byte_ns))
    print(
        "────────────────────────────────────"
    )
end

vim.keymap.set("n", "<leader><leader>", iter_lua_fn)
```

Result from running on this file:

```
=== line() Performance Comparison ===
Positions     : ., $, 'a, ., $, 'b, ., $, "
Total calls   : 18000000
────────────────────────────────────
vim.call      : 7556410.76 µs total  (   420 ns/call)
lua_fn      : 6205447.78 µs total  (   345 ns/call)
────────────────────────────────────
lua_fn vs vim : 1.22x faster
────────────────────────────────────

=== line() Performance Comparison ===
Positions     : ., $, 'a, ., $, 'b, ., $, "
Total calls   : 18000000
────────────────────────────────────
vim.call      : 7466809.33 µs total  (   415 ns/call)
lua_fn      : 6197159.22 µs total  (   344 ns/call)
────────────────────────────────────
lua_fn vs vim : 1.20x faster
────────────────────────────────────

=== line() Performance Comparison ===
Positions     : ., $, 'a, ., $, 'b, ., $, "
Total calls   : 18000000
────────────────────────────────────
vim.call      : 7462791.94 µs total  (   415 ns/call)
lua_fn      : 6137708.81 µs total  (   341 ns/call)
────────────────────────────────────
lua_fn vs vim : 1.22x faster
────────────────────────────────────
```

Seems like there's opportunity here. On the other hand, there would need to be an API method to get topline and botline.

(Disclosure: outputs AI assisted)

EDIT: If you pass the current window explicitly (forcing a call to nvim_win_get_buf()):

```
=== line() Performance Comparison ===
Positions     : ., $, 'a, ., $, 'b, ., $, "
Total calls   : 18000000
────────────────────────────────────
vim.call      : 9151163.38 µs total  (   508 ns/call)
lua_fn      : 7658284.20 µs total  (   425 ns/call)
────────────────────────────────────
lua_fn vs vim : 1.19x faster
────────────────────────────────────

=== line() Performance Comparison ===
Positions     : ., $, 'a, ., $, 'b, ., $, "
Total calls   : 18000000
────────────────────────────────────
vim.call      : 9139799.51 µs total  (   508 ns/call)
lua_fn      : 7698417.28 µs total  (   428 ns/call)
────────────────────────────────────
lua_fn vs vim : 1.19x faster
────────────────────────────────────

=== line() Performance Comparison ===
Positions     : ., $, 'a, ., $, 'b, ., $, "
Total calls   : 18000000
────────────────────────────────────
vim.call      : 9155046.58 µs total  (   509 ns/call)
lua_fn      : 7760013.75 µs total  (   431 ns/call)
────────────────────────────────────
lua_fn vs vim : 1.18x faster
────────────────────────────────────
```
Sounds like no objections. Next PR replaces `fn.environ()` with a full-Lua impl: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39176

title: perf(vim.fn): call Lua-implemented vim.fn.xx() directly
